title: Premises and Facility Requirements for an MIA
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A senior QP guide to MIA premises requirements: EU GMP layout, segregation, Annex 1 cleanrooms, utilities and the ALCOA+ evidence MHRA inspectors expect.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury banner of a pristine pharmaceutical cleanroom corridor, gleaming stainless-steel airlock doors and seamless epoxy flooring receding into soft focus, lit by cool teal and cyan ambient light against deep navy shadows, a single subtle green indicator glow on a wall-mounted pressure gauge, faint condensation of vapour catching soft natural daylight from an unseen window, shallow depth of field with the nearest door handle crisp and the far corridor melting into bokeh, calm sterile atmosphere conveying precision and control, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos, no people.

faqs.q: Do non-sterile MIA facilities need cleanroom classification?
faqs.a: Formal Grade A to D classification under Annex 1 applies to sterile manufacture, not to all non-sterile facilities. However, non-sterile premises still require defined and justified environmental conditions appropriate to the product, such as controlled temperature, humidity and air quality. The level of control should be driven by a quality risk assessment under ICH Q9 and documented accordingly.

faqs.q: When do I need a dedicated facility rather than shared premises?
faqs.a: Certain highly sensitising products, such as some beta-lactam antibiotics, generally require dedicated and self-contained facilities under EU GMP Chapter 3. For other products, a toxicological evaluation using health-based exposure limits determines whether shared facilities and equipment can be justified. Where shared use is proposed, robust cleaning validation and cross-contamination controls must support that decision.

faqs.q: What premises evidence does an MHRA pre-approval inspection focus on?
faqs.a: Inspectors check that the building suits the activities declared on your licence application and that design choices are justified through quality risk management. They expect to see qualification documentation, including URS, DQ and IQ/OQ/PQ, plus utility qualification, environmental monitoring data and maintenance records. The supporting records must meet ALCOA+ data integrity principles to be considered reliable.
